title: SemiAnalysis Energy Model
gist: A product description for the SemiAnalysis Energy Model, an institutional dataset that translates AI datacenter buildout into US power-infrastructure demand and forecasts where the grid falls short. It is a methodology/marketing page, not an analytical piece — no specific forecasts or data points are disclosed.

insights.claim: SemiAnalysis frames AI datacenter buildout primarily as a power-infrastructure demand problem, modeling supply-demand gaps across major US electricity markets rather than treating compute capacity in isolation.
insights.speaker: SemiAnalysis
insights.so_what: Signals that the binding constraint on AI scaling is electricity supply, not chips — watch utilities, IPPs, and grid buildout as the AI-capex chokepoint.
insights.quote: The SemiAnalysis Energy Model translates AI datacenter buildout into power infrastructure demand, providing a granular view of the supply-demand gaps across major electricity markets in the United States.

insights.claim: SemiAnalysis builds its grid-gap forecast by integrating a national inventory of generators, storage, and onsite energy with datacenter IT capacity projections and utility/RTO/ISO integrated resource plans.
insights.speaker: SemiAnalysis
insights.so_what: The methodology cross-checks SemiAnalysis demand estimates against utilities' own forecasts, useful for spotting where utility plans under- or over-state coming AI load.
insights.quote: We integrate a national inventory of electric generators, storage, and onsite energy solutions with datacenter IT capacity projections and integrated resource plans from electric utilities, RTOs, and ISOs.

insights.claim: SemiAnalysis asserts that power supply and transmission constraints — not demand — may delay datacenter load growth, and the model is built to identify where those constraints can be overcome.
insights.speaker: SemiAnalysis
insights.so_what: Implies AI load growth is supply-gated regionally; watch transmission and firm-capacity buildout by utility as the gating factor for hyperscaler expansion.
insights.quote: where power supply and transmission constraints may delay load growth

insights.claim: The model converts existing and planned generation into 'firm capacity' and tracks ESAs and LOAs, indicating that nameplate generation overstates what is actually deliverable to datacenter loads.
insights.speaker: SemiAnalysis
insights.so_what: Firm (deliverable) capacity, not nameplate, is the right metric for judging which datacenters can actually be powered — a screen for stranded-buildout risk.
insights.quote: Inventory of existing and planned generation, translated to firm capacity

insights.claim: SemiAnalysis sells the Energy Model to utilities, IPPs, equipment manufacturers, hyperscalers, datacenter developers, and public/private market investors, signaling cross-sector demand for AI-power supply intelligence.
insights.speaker: SemiAnalysis
insights.so_what: The breadth of buyers confirms AI-driven power demand is now a mainstream investment thesis spanning energy and tech, not a niche datacenter concern.
insights.quote: Our clients include utilities, IPPs (Independent Power Producers), equipment manufacturers, hyperscalers, datacenter developers, and large investors in public and private markets.
